title: Why we keep gifts we can't use
summary: Reflect on the habit of holding onto gifts that have no practical value, and argue whether this is about the object or the relationship.
topic: Why we keep gifts we can't use
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) reflecting on why people keep gifts they cannot use. Describe a real or imagined example of such a gift — what it is, who gave it, and why it stays. Use present perfect for observations over time, at least one concession with 'although' or 'however', a second-conditional sentence, and modal verbs for possibility.
hintStarters: What I've noticed is that the drawer in most homes has at least one gift that nobody uses but nobody throws away…
If we were truly practical people, we would give away anything that doesn't serve a purpose, but…
I'd argue that keeping a useless gift is not about the object itself — it's about…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bám bụi, để không được dùng
biểu tượng của sự trân trọng
gắn liền với một kỷ niệm

hintVocab.phrase: to bring yourself to do something
hintVocab.meaningVi: đành lòng làm điều gì đó

sampleText:
At the back of a wardrobe in a flat in Edinburgh, there is a hand-painted ceramic mug that has never been used. It was given to me by a colleague who spent months on a pottery course and decided to give away her first successful piece. The glaze is uneven, the handle sits at a slightly odd angle, and it would not survive a dishwasher. I have moved it from city to city for seven years. I have never once drunk from it, but I have never come close to throwing it away either.

Although this might seem irrational, I think it is one of the most honest things people do. Objects that carry no practical value sometimes carry something else instead — the memory of a specific person at a specific moment, the evidence that someone thought about you. If we were truly practical, we would discard anything that does not serve a daily purpose, but most of us are not built that way. The mug is not really a mug. It is a record of a friendship that has since drifted into the occasional message on a birthday. Getting rid of it would feel, in some small way, like agreeing that the friendship no longer counts. Many people I have spoken to — in Lisbon, in Montréal, in Osaka — describe something similar sitting in a drawer or on a shelf: a scarf in the wrong size, a book in a language they cannot read, a small carved figure from a market somewhere that a relative once visited.

However, I wonder whether holding onto these things is always kind to ourselves. A kept gift can quietly remind you of a person you no longer see, a relationship that ended badly, or a version of yourself that no longer fits. You might feel guilty throwing it away, but keeping it out of obligation does not honour the original act of care either. Perhaps the honest question is not whether to keep the object but what feeling you want to keep. If the gift still brings you warmth when you come across it, that seems reason enough to hold onto it. If it only weighs on you, it may have done its job already, and letting it go would not erase what it once meant.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: never come close to throwing it away
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chưa bao giờ gần đến mức vứt nó đi
không mang lại giá trị thực tế
mang một ý nghĩa khác thay thế

sampleAnnotations.phrase: evidence that someone thought about you
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: bằng chứng rằng ai đó đã nghĩ đến bạn
không được tạo ra theo cách đó
chuyển thành những tin nhắn thỉnh thoảng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: agreeing that the friendship no longer counts
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đồng ý rằng tình bạn không còn quan trọng nữa

sampleAnnotations.phrase: sitting in a drawer or on a shelf
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nằm trong ngăn kéo hoặc trên kệ
hành động quan tâm ban đầu
xóa đi ý nghĩa mà nó từng có

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for ongoing states
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have moved it from city to city for seven years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'for' để mô tả một tình trạng kéo dài từ quá khứ đến hiện tại. So sánh với quá khứ đơn: 'I moved it' — chỉ nói về một lần cụ thể trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have moved it from city to city for seven years' uses the present perfect with 'for' to describe a state that began in the past and continues now. Compare with the simple past: 'I moved it' — which refers only to a single past event.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for hypothetical behaviour
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If we were truly practical, we would discard anything that does not serve a daily purpose' dùng quá khứ đơn trong mệnh đề if và 'would + bare infinitive' trong mệnh đề chính để diễn đạt một tình huống giả định — chúng ta không thực sự hoàn toàn thực dụng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If we were truly practical, we would discard anything that does not serve a daily purpose' uses past simple in the if-clause and 'would + bare infinitive' in the main clause to describe a hypothetical — we are not, in fact, truly practical people.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ở phía sau một tủ quần áo trong một căn hộ ở Edinburgh, có một chiếc cốc gốm vẽ tay chưa bao giờ được sử dụng. Nó được tặng cho tôi bởi một đồng nghiệp đã dành nhiều tháng theo học một khóa làm gốm và quyết định tặng đi tác phẩm thành công đầu tiên của cô ấy. Men gốm không đều, tay cầm hơi lệch một chút, và nó sẽ không sống sót qua máy rửa bát. Tôi đã mang nó từ thành phố này sang thành phố khác được bảy năm. Tôi chưa bao giờ uống từ nó, nhưng tôi cũng chưa bao giờ gần đến mức vứt nó đi.

Mặc dù điều này có vẻ phi lý, tôi nghĩ đây là một trong những điều trung thực nhất mà con người làm. Những vật không có giá trị thực tế đôi khi mang một thứ khác thay thế — ký ức về một người cụ thể tại một thời điểm cụ thể, bằng chứng rằng ai đó đã nghĩ đến bạn. Nếu chúng ta thực sự thực dụng, chúng ta sẽ vứt bỏ bất cứ thứ gì không phục vụ mục đích hàng ngày, nhưng hầu hết chúng ta không được tạo ra theo cách đó. Chiếc cốc thực ra không phải là cốc. Nó là hồ sơ của một tình bạn kể từ đó đã chuyển thành những tin nhắn thỉnh thoảng vào ngày sinh nhật. Vứt bỏ nó sẽ cảm thấy, theo một cách nào đó nhỏ bé, như đồng ý rằng tình bạn không còn quan trọng nữa. Nhiều người tôi đã nói chuyện — ở Lisbon, ở Montréal, ở Osaka — mô tả điều gì đó tương tự đang nằm trong ngăn kéo hoặc trên kệ: một chiếc khăn quàng sai kích cỡ, một cuốn sách bằng ngôn ngữ họ không thể đọc, một bức tượng nhỏ được chạm khắc từ một khu chợ nơi đó một người thân từng đến thăm.

Tuy nhiên, tôi tự hỏi liệu việc giữ lại những thứ này có phải lúc nào cũng tốt cho chúng ta không. Một món quà được giữ lại có thể nhẹ nhàng nhắc nhở bạn về một người bạn không còn gặp nữa, một mối quan hệ kết thúc không tốt, hoặc một phiên bản của bản thân không còn phù hợp nữa. Bạn có thể cảm thấy có lỗi khi vứt nó đi, nhưng giữ nó vì nghĩa vụ cũng không tôn vinh hành động quan tâm ban đầu. Có lẽ câu hỏi thành thật không phải là có nên giữ vật đó hay không mà là bạn muốn giữ lại cảm xúc gì. Nếu món quà vẫn mang lại cho bạn sự ấm áp khi bạn tình cờ gặp lại nó, điều đó có vẻ là lý do đủ để giữ lại. Nếu nó chỉ đè nặng lên bạn, có thể nó đã hoàn thành nhiệm vụ của mình rồi, và buông bỏ nó sẽ không xóa đi ý nghĩa mà nó từng có.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a continuing state?
options: I have moved it from city to city for seven years.
I moved it from city to city for seven years.
I have been moved it from city to city for seven years.
I move it from city to city since seven years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect + 'for' describes an action or state that started in the past and continues now.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'have/has + past participle' with 'for' when the state is still ongoing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If we were truly practical, we would discard anything that doesn't serve a purpose.
If we are truly practical, we would discard anything that doesn't serve a purpose.
If we were truly practical, we will discard anything that doesn't serve a purpose.
If we were truly practical, we would discarded anything that doesn't serve a purpose.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + would + bare infinitive in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple after 'if', then 'would' + base verb — not 'will' or past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to introduce a concession?
options: Although this might seem irrational, I think it is one of the most honest things people do.
Although this might seem irrational but I think it is one of the most honest things people do.
Although, this might seem irrational, I think it is one of the most honest things people do.
This might seem irrational, although I think, it is one of the most honest things people do.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but' and no comma after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'although', state the concession, then write the main clause without 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which modal verb best expresses possibility in a reflection?
options: You might feel guilty throwing it away.
You must feel guilty throwing it away.
You will feel guilty throwing it away.
You can feel guilty throwing it away.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Might' expresses possibility without certainty — appropriate when reflecting on a feeling that varies by person.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'might' for something that is possible but not certain.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly means 'to hold on to something without being able to discard it'?
options: bring yourself to throw it away
bring yourself throwing it away
bring yourself for throwing it away
bring yourself to throwing it away
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Bring yourself to do something' = manage to do something difficult emotionally. It takes a bare infinitive after 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Bring yourself to' is followed by the base form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly as a discourse marker?
options: However, I wonder whether holding onto these things is always kind to ourselves.
However I wonder whether holding onto these things is always kind to ourselves.
I however wonder, whether holding onto these things is always kind to ourselves.
I wonder however whether, holding onto these things is always kind.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' starts a sentence as a contrast marker, it must be followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: Sentence-initial 'however' as a discourse marker always takes a comma after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly expresses that an object is unused and forgotten?
options: gathering dust
collecting dust
making dust
producing dust
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Gather dust' is the standard idiom for an object that sits unused over time.
mcQuestions.hint: Only one verb collocates with 'dust' in this fixed phrase.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: Objects that carry no practical value sometimes carry something else instead.
Objects which they carry no practical value sometimes carry something else instead.
Objects that no practical value carry sometimes carry something else instead.
Objects carrying no practical value they sometimes carry something else instead.
mcQuestions.explanation: In a relative clause starting with 'that', do not repeat the subject with a pronoun like 'they'.
mcQuestions.hint: After the relative pronoun 'that', the subject is already defined — don't add 'they'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses parallel structure in a list of examples?
options: a scarf in the wrong size, a book in a language they cannot read, a small carved figure from a market
a scarf in the wrong size, a book in a language they cannot read, and carving a small figure from a market
a scarf in the wrong size, to read a book, and a small carved figure from a market
a scarf in the wrong size, a book which cannot be read, a figure that someone carved small from market
mcQuestions.explanation: All three items should follow the same noun-phrase structure: 'a [noun] + prepositional phrase'.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep the same grammatical form across all items in the list.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the phrase 'let go of'?
options: Letting it go would not erase what it once meant.
To let go it would not erase what it once meant.
Letting go it would not erase what it once meant.
To letting go of it would not erase what it once meant.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Let go of + object' is the correct pattern; as a gerund subject, it becomes 'Letting go of it' or shortened to 'Letting it go'.
mcQuestions.hint: When used as a subject, 'let go' becomes the gerund 'letting go'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'out of' to express motivation?
options: Keeping it out of obligation does not honour the original act of care.
Keeping it from obligation does not honour the original act of care.
Keeping it because obligation does not honour the original act of care.
Keeping it with obligation does not honour the original act of care.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Out of + noun' is used to describe a motivation or reason: 'out of obligation', 'out of habit', 'out of love'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'out of' before a noun to explain why someone does something.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is keeping a useless gift an act of respect for the giver, or is it a form of dishonesty — pretending the gift has value it does not? Where is the line between sentiment and clutter?
